reason: Closed-book solvability failure: multiple options labeled Correct (A, C, D) rely on paper-specific empirical findings not provided in the stem (e.g., explicit causal attribution of window shortening to SoC-dependent threshold temperatures, H2 being absent during safety venting across all SoC, and the strong SoC-dependence of H2 during thermal runaway). These cannot be derived from the stem’s given timing and total-volume comparisons plus general engineering reasoning, so the correct/incorrect labeling depends on hidden literature details.

MCQ; papers provided. All Correct options (A,C,D,F) are directly supported by facts stated in the stem and/or derivable by general reasoning (e.g., higher pressure per liter implies less volume for same threshold), and none contradict the evidence bundle. Incorrect options (B,E) are unambiguously refuted by the stem/evidence (damage occurred ~50% below Jarrett/Baker minor-damage thresholds; peak overpressure is non-monotonic with volume due to venting through the door). No hidden paper-only definitions are required beyond what the stem explicitly provides.

MCQ; papers provided. All Correct-labeled options (A,C,D,E) are directly supported by information explicitly stated in the stem and align with the evidence bundle (fluorinated compounds do not affect ignition delay; ignition delay insensitive to oxygen content; carbonate mixtures ignite up to 10× slower than H2/CO-rich at 730°C; ignition strongly temperature-dependent with ~550°C noted). Incorrect-labeled options (B,F) are rejectable using general combustion reasoning plus the stem (burning velocity ≠ ignition delay; no basis to attribute higher burning velocity to fluorinated content). No ambiguity detected and no contradiction with the evidence.

Short-answer item; papers provided. The stem plus general mechanism reasoning makes it solvable: evidence anchors that CH4 is most abundant during safety venting and increases with SoC, H2 is not detected during safety venting, H2 appears during thermal runaway and increases strongly with SoC, and the venting-to-TR detection window shrinks at high SoC (e.g., 142 s at 100% SoC). The gold answer’s core claims rely only on these stated relationships (no hidden definitions or thresholds), are internally coherent, non-ambiguous, and not contradicted by the evidence bundle.
